prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's a first-generation college student. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's a first-generation college student. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's a first-generation college student. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

However, the prompt asks for a grade and feedback, implying an assessment of quality.
    *   **Topic:** "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned."
    *   **Format:** Short personal narrative (~500 words).
    *   **Desired Output:** Grade out of 10, brief feedback on "what works" and "what doesn't."

2.  **Analyze the Essay:**
    *   **Word Count:** It looks to be around 350-400 words. The prompt asks for ~500 words. It's a bit short, but feels complete.
    *   **Content/Story:**
        *   *Beginning:* Sets the scene (8th grade basketball tryouts). Establishes hubris/confidence (driveway practice, beating Marcus).
        *   *Middle:* The realization of failure (other kids better, didn't know plays). Specific details (pick and roll, fast break). The emotional low point (scrimmage, "stay out of the way").
        *   *Climax/Resolution:* The cut list. Marcus made it; narrator didn't. Comparison.
        *   *Reflection:* Pivoting to debate. Finding strength elsewhere.
        *   *Analysis/Deepening:* Distinguishing casual vs. competitive skill. The realization of "you don't know what you don't know."
        *   *Conclusion:* Failure as information.
    *   **Style/Voice:** Conversational, honest, slightly colloquial ("So yeah," "Obviously"). Reads like a student speaking naturally.
    *   **Strengths:**
        *   Clear narrative arc.
        *   Specific details (pick and roll, fast break, Marcus).
        *   Honest vulnerability (admitting to being out of league).
        *   Insightful takeaway (failure as information, casual vs. competitive, Dunning-Kruger effect essentially).
    *   **Weaknesses:**
        *   *Length:* It's under the 500-word goal, though not critically short. It could use more elaboration.
        *   *Ending:* The last paragraph is a bit abrupt ("So yeah..."). It summarizes rather than showing a lingering thought or imagery.
        *   *Structure:* It's very linear. "I did this, then this, then I learned this." It's functional but could be more engaging with a stronger hook or reflective integration.
        *   *First-Gen Context:* Does the essay *address* this? No. Does the *feedback* need to address this? The prompt mentions he *is* a first-gen student. Usually, admissions essays might touch on this, but this specific prompt is generic ("fail at something"). I should treat the essay on its own merits regarding the prompt given, but perhaps keep the tone supportive, as first-gen students often undervalue their own writing abilities.

3.  **Determine the Grade:**
    *   It's a solid B to B+ paper. It answers the prompt clearly. It has a beginning, middle, and end. The reflection is mature.
    *   However, it's not "A" material in a creative writing sense because it's a bit plain. It lacks a "spark" or a really unique narrative voice. It tells rather than shows in a few places.
    *   Scale out of 10: 7.5 or 8. Let's go with **8/10**. It's a competent, well-structured response to the prompt, even if it's not literary gold.

4.  **Drafting the Feedback:**
    *   *What Works:* The honesty, the specific details (pick and roll), the self-awareness in the reflection. The transition to debate is a classic "pivotal moment" trope but executed well here.
    *   *What Doesn't / Areas for Improvement:*
        *   Length/Depth: A little short. Could expand on the feelings during the scrimmage or the debate transition.
        *   Ending: The final paragraph is a bit generic ("Failure is just information"). It's a good point, but could be woven in better or ended with a stronger image.
        *   The "So yeah": Too informal for an academic essay, though maybe fine for a casual personal narrative.

5.  **Refining the Feedback for the User:**
    *   Be constructive.
    *   Acknowledge the specific strengths (the realization of the "Dunning-Kruger" aspect).
    *   Address the length/depth issue gently.

6.  **Review against "First-Generation College Student" context:**
    *   Sometimes first-gen students worry their "boring" lives or lack of fancy vocabulary hurt them. This essay proves that doesn't matter here. It's relatable. I should emphasize that the *authenticity* is the strength here.
    *   I will frame the feedback to encourage his natural voice while pointing out where he can deepen the narrative.

7.  **Final Polish of the Output:**
    *   *Grade:* 8/10.
    *   *What works:* Honest voice, clear narrative arc, insightful reflection (specifically the difference between casual and competitive).
    *   *What could be better:* Show, don't tell (expand on the "annoyance" of the other kids), smooth out the ending (remove "So yeah"), maybe add a bit more detail on the debate part to show *why* it was a better fit (contrast the feeling).

    *(Self-Correction during drafting)*: I initially thought the ending was weak, but "failure is just information" is actually a very strong, mature thesis statement for a college student. It shows resilience. I shouldn't criticize the *content* of the ending too much, just the phrasing ("So yeah").

8.  **Final Output Generation.** (Matches the drafted plan).

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's a first-generation college student. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's a first-generation college student. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's a first-generation college student. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

### **Grade: 8/10**

This is a solid, relatable essay. It is honest, well-structured, and clearly answers the prompt. It reads like a genuine personal reflection rather than a generic attempt to impress the reader.

### **What Works**
*   **Authentic Voice:** The voice is very natural and grounded. The admission that the other kid "wasn't wrong" shows a lot of maturity and self-awareness, which makes the essay feel sincere.
*   **Strong Insight:** The distinction between "casual" and "competitive" skill levels is excellent. It’s a specific realization that goes deeper than the usual "I learned to work hard" cliché found in these types of essays.
*   **Great Closing:** The final sentence ("failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information") is a strong, punchy summary that ties the whole piece together perfectly.

### **What Could Be Improved**
*   **Show, Don’t Tell:** The essay is a little light on sensory details. Instead of just saying the other kids were "faster and taller," describe what it felt like to stand next to them or how fast the game moved compared to the driveway.
*   **The Pivot to Debate:** The transition from basketball to debate feels a little rushed. Why debate specifically? Was there a specific moment or reason he chose that, or was it just random? Connecting those dots would make the narrative feel more intentional.
